Target grid (T):

Row1: X X X X □   => cells (1,1)-(1,4) X; (1,5) □. So leftmost 4 columns X.
Row2: X □ □ □ □  => cell (2,1) X; rest empty.

Distractor 1 (D1):

Row2 same.
Row3 same.
Row4: □ □ □ X □  => cells: (4,4) X only; rest empty.
Row5 same.

Distractor 2 (D2):

Row1: □ X X X X  => (1,2)-(1,5) X; (1,1) empty.

Row4: □ X □ □ X => (4,2) X; (4,5) X.

So target grid has leftmost column mostly X (except row2 only X). Also top two rows 1 & 3 have left 4 X; row4 has X at col1 and col5; row5 left 4 X. So shape looks like vertical bar on left plus maybe a rightmost X on row4. Distinct from D1 which has only X at (4,4). D1 also has same leftmost pattern. D2 has leftmost column all empty, but second column X all rows.

Thus to distinguish target, we need mention leftmost column majority X, also mention rightmost column at row4 maybe. So expression could be: "Four consecutive rows have X in the first four columns, except the second row has only X in the first column" etc.

But we also want clarity. Perhaps: "The grid has X in a 4x3 block covering rows 1,3,5 and columns 1-4, plus an extra X in the last column of row 4." Wait D1 has X in columns 1-4 rows 1,3,5 same, but D1 lacks X at last column row4; D1 has X at (4,4). Actually D1's X at (4,4) inside that block not outside. So difference: D1 has X at (4,4) but target has nothing there. So we can describe that target does NOT have X at (4,4). Instead has X at (4,5). So referencing that will differentiate.

Thus expression: "An X in the first column of all rows except row2, a 4×3 block of X in rows 1,3,5 and columns 1–4, and an extra X in the last column of row 4." But we can refine: "All cells in the first column are X except the cell at row 2, column 1?" Wait row2 col1 is X, so all cells in first column are X. So simply "All cells in first column are X." Yes.

Then "Rows 1,3,5 have X in columns 1–4." And "Row 4 has X in columns 1 and 5." Distinguish.

D1 had row4 none except 4; but still column1 is X, fine. But difference is row4 has X at 4 in D1, but not in target. So we could state: "No X at row4,column4." In target, only X at col5.

Thus expression: "Column 1 is all X; rows 1,3,5 have X in columns 1‑4; row 4 has X in columns 1 and 5 (and no X at column 4)."

Also must emphasize "except at row4 column4." But not mention others.

Simplify to: "Column 1 contains all X; rows 1,3,5 contain X in columns 1–4; the only X in row 4 outside the first column is in column 5." That should uniquely identify.

Let's produce expression: "Expression: The first column is fully X, rows 1, 3, 5 have X in columns 1‑4, and row 4 has X only in columns 1 and 5 (no X at column 4)." That sounds fine.

Let's produce final output.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>Expression: The first column is entirely X; rows 1, 3, 5 have X only in columns 1‑4; row 4 has X only in columns 1 and 5 (with no X at column 4).<|return|>
